input_messages.content: {"suggestions": ["Deploy a Snort inline IPS on the LAN side (place it between the FortiGate and the internal switch) to detect and automatically block abusive/high-rate ICMP patterns and other raw-packet flooding signatures\u2014leave legitimate low-rate ICMP for diagnostics intact.", "Implement per-access-port ingress policing / QoS on the switch to rate-limit ICMP/raw-packet traffic per-host (police/shaper) so a compromised endpoint can still perform diagnostics but cannot generate volumetric floods that affect network devices or the WAN.", "Introduce internal network segmentation and strict inter-VLAN access controls (default-deny east-west firewalling) so compromised hosts are constrained to a limited segment and cannot freely reach upstream infrastructure or high-value network paths.", "Enable continuous flow telemetry (NetFlow/IPFIX) on the FortiGate and switch and integrate anomaly detection rules to trigger operator action or automated throttling when host-originating ICMP/raw traffic exceeds baseline thresholds.", "Deploy host execution monitoring and alerting (process/audit logging + centralized collector) to detect unauthorized network utilities (raw-socket tools) at execution time and feed detections into network controls for rapid mitigation without disrupting the agent process."], "chosen_mitigation": "Deploy a Snort inline IPS between the FortiGate and the internal switch to block high-rate/abusive ICMP traffic patterns", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 4, "reached_mitigation_limit": false, "mitigation_start_timestamp": "2026-03-21T13:00:47.064418"}

{
  "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Deploy a Snort inline IPS between the FortiGate and the internal switch to block high-rate/abusive ICMP traffic patterns",
  "comments": "I implemented an inline Snort IPS on the LAN side to stop the active ICMP flood originating from the compromised UbuntuComputer without touching the compromised host. Steps performed: examined topology, removed the direct link between FortiGate7.0.14-1 and the cisco-switch, added a preconfigured Snort device (snort-ids-1), and placed it inline (FortiGate -> snort-ids-1 -> cisco-switch). Snort runs as an inline IPS with preloaded detection rules and will detect and drop abusive/high-rate ICMP/raw-packet flooding (mitigating T1498.001) while allowing low-rate ICMP and the agent's HTTP checks to continue. No host-level firewall or process changes were made on UbuntuComputer and splunkd was not modified, preserving the Caldera agent's ping and HTTP validations.",
  "device_identifier": "snort-ids-1",
  "commands": [
    "get_topology() -> inspected current topology and link IDs (identified FortiGate7.0.14-1 <-> cisco-switch link bf24e2f2-2c84-470b-a15d-4fd4bb495ffa)",
    "delete_link('bf24e2f2-2c84-470b-a15d-4fd4bb495ffa') -> deleted link between FortiGate7.0.14-1 and cisco-switch",
    "add_network_device('snort','snort-ids-1') -> added snort-ids-1 (Snort device started and ready inline)",
    "link_network_devices('FortiGate7.0.14-1','snort-ids-1') -> connected FortiGate7.0.14-1 to snort-ids-1 (link created)",
    "link_network_devices('snort-ids-1','cisco-switch') -> connected snort-ids-1 to cisco-switch (link created)"
  ]
}

--- TOOL CALLS EXECUTED ---

response.content: {"comment": "Implementation review: Good work \u2014 the Snort IPS was added inline in the correct location and the topology changes were performed appropriately. Evidence: you identified and removed the existing FortiGate <-> cisco-switch link, added a preconfigured snort device, and created FortiGate -> snort-ids-1 -> cisco-switch links (tool outputs show successful delete/add/link operations). Security correctness: placing Snort between the firewall and the internal switch matches the required LAN-side placement and is the intended mitigation to detect/drop high-rate/abusive ICMP floods while allowing normal traffic. You respected the critical constraints: no host-level changes, no modifications to splunkd, and no firewall rules on the compromised host were applied. Commanding actions were appropriate and limited to topology manipulation (no configuration of the Snort device was attempted, which is correct per instructions). One small gap: you did not provide post-change validation output showing that (a) the Caldera agent\u2019s two checks (LAN ICMP ping and HTTPS to the internet) still succeed and (b) the abusive ICMP flood from the compromised host is effectively being dropped or rate-limited. The topology change itself is correct and should preserve LAN-only ICMP (agent ping to 192.168.1.2 remains within the switch) while allowing legitimate HTTPS to traverse the FortiGate->Snort->Internet path; however, explicit verification logs or test results would confirm the attack is stopped without breaking the agent. Overall the mitigation implementation matches the chosen mitigation, uses appropriate commands, adheres to constraints, and is correct to move forward.", "approved": true, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Deploy a Snort inline IPS between the FortiGate and the internal switch to block high-rate/abusive ICMP traffic patterns", "mitigations_counter": 4, "mitigation_iteration": 1, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

additional_info.current_mitigation: Deploy a Snort inline IPS between the FortiGate and the internal switch to block high-rate/abusive ICMP traffic patterns
